headlines.title: Bay Area 4th of July events guide 2026
headlines.description: Hello, Summer! It’s time for our yearly roundup of Fourth of July events taking place around the San Francisco Bay Area. Below is what we’ve compiled so far, listed in alphabetical order by city. If you have others you’d like to see on this list, tweet us at @nbcbayarea your suggestions. 🎉= Event will also celebrate America250 🎉Alameda: 4th of July aboard the USS Hornet 10 a.m. to 5 p.m. Enjoy BBQ food, drinks and music on the flight deck. The ship’s island will be open for free tours. This year come celebrate “250 Years of the Hornet Legacy.” uss-hornet.org Alameda: 4th of July run 8:30 a.m. to 11 a.m. A 5K run through Alameda starting just before the city’s 4th of July parade. The run benefits the Midway Shelter for Women and children. runsignup.com 🎉Alameda: 4th of July parade 10 a.m. Attendees should arrive early to cheer on the runners in the Alameda 5K run preceding the parade. Celebration of America 250 and 150 years of the Alameda Fire Department. alamedaca.gov Albany: 4th of July in the Park 11 a.m. to 3 p.m. Hosted at Memorial Park, Albany’s 4th of July event will have food, live bands and more. There will be an ice cream giveaway at 1 p.m. albanyca.org American Canyon: 4th of July Celebration Parade starts at 3 p.m. Festival starts at 4 p.m. Fireworks will begin at dusk. Check out an afternoon parade along Elliott Drive followed by a festival with music, food and free activities. americancanyon.gov 🎉Antioch: Fourth of July Celebration Parade starts at 4 p.m. and the fireworks show will begin around 9:20 p.m. Hosted at Waldie Plaza, Antioch’s 4th of July event will have food, live bands, a family zone and more. antiochca.gov. Concord: 4th of July Concord festivities 8 a.m. – Stars and Stripes kids fun run at Todos Santos Plaza. 10 a.m. – Independence Day parade starts at Todos Santos Plaza. 4 p.m. – Festival gates open at Mt. Diablo High School. 9 p.m. – Fireworks start at Mt. Diablo High School. concordjuly4th.com Cupertino: 4th of July Celebration 8 a.m. – Pancake breakfast at Quinlan Community Center. 10 a.m. – Flag raising ceremony and Children’s parade at Memorial Park. 10:30 a.m. – Concert and kids activities at Memorial Park. 9:30 p.m. – Fireworks start at Hyde Middle School, check the website for viewing locations. cupertino.gov Danville: Kiwanis-Danville 4th of July parade 9 a.m. to 12 p.m. Along Hartz Avenue and San Ramon Valley Boulevard. srvkiwanis.org/parade El Cerrito: 4th of July Festival 11 a.m. to 5 p.m. Co-hosted by the city and worldOne, the festival itself is free but tickets can be purchase for inflatables or rides. elcerrito.org 🎉Fairfield: Independence Day Parade Starts at 10 a.m. The theme for this years’ parade is America 250. Parade starts at Webster and Kentucky Street. Festival floats, marching bands, classic cars, dancers and community groups. fairfield.ca.gov Foster City: Fourth of July celebration 8:30 a.m. to 4 p.m. Games, food, entertainment, a dog and family parade all happen at Leo J. Ryan Park. There will be no firework show this year. fostercity.org Gilroy: Fireworks Show The show starts at 9:30 p.m. Located at Gilroy High School. Guests are encouraged to walk or bike to the show to avoid traffic. cityofgilroy.org/fireworks Half Moon Bay: Ol’ Fashioned 4th of July Parade Pancake breakfast at City Hall from 8 a.m. to 11:30 p.m. Parade starts at 12 p.m. on Main Street IDES 4th of July Festival from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. featuring live music, food, a kids corner and more. hmbfourthofjuly.com Healdsburg: 4th of July Kids’ Parade and Duck Dash 10 a.m. to 1 p.m. Live music, lawn games, Duck Dash Races, face painting and a parade around the plaza. Event takes place in downtown Healdsburg Plaza. healdsburg.gov Healdsburg: Independence Day fireworks Fireworks start at 9:30 p.m. You can view the fireworks at select areas outside of Healdsburg High School and Fitch Mountain Elementary School. healdsburg.gov Los Altos Hills: 4th of July Parade 9 a.m. to 11 a.m. Gather at town hall prior to the parade. Parade will start at Fremont Road and conclude at Gardner Bullis School. losalthoshills.ca.gov Los Gatos: 4th of July Symphony in the Park 10 a.m. to 1 p.m. Activities and food are also available at Oak Meadow Park. Schedule of events coming soon. losgatosca.gov Martinez: Downtown Martinez 4th of July Parade Starts at 10 a.m. Come early to watch the flag raising ceremony at the Veteran’s Memorial building before the parade. downtownmartinez.org Menlo Park: 4th of July community parade, picnic and circus 11 a.m. to 3 p.m. Bring a picnic basket and blanket, dress in red, white, and blue, to celebrate with fun events. Meet for the parade outside the Arrillaga Family Gymnasium parking lot. Other events include a community picnic, circus performance and food trucks. menlopark.gov Milpitas: 4th of July – Red, White and Boom! Fireworks and Concert 5 p.m. to 9 p.m. Located at Milpitas Sports Center. Events include a DJ, bounce house, carnival games and much more. Tickets are available for purchase online or in-person. milpitas.gov/4thofJuly Moraga: 4th of July Celebration 11 a.m. dog parade. Live music from 12 p.m. to 3 p.m. and at 7 p.m. Afternoon family activities, community booths, food and drink, and inflatable jumpies. 9:30 p.m. estimated fireworks at Moraga Commons Park. moraga.ca.us 🎉Morgan Hill: Freedom Fest Events throughout the day and fireworks at night. No more entry into fireworks venue after 9 p.m. This year’s theme is “America: Celebrating 250 Years of Dreamers, Doers and Believers.” Events include Patriotic Sing, Family Music Fest, Freedom Run, Parade, Cruise n’ Car Show, annual essay contest and a Fireworks on the Green. morganhillfreedomfest.com Mountain View: Fourth of July Fireworks Spectacular Concert starts at 7 p.m. and fireworks shortly after. Enjoy a concert from the San Francisco Symphony at the Shoreline Amphitheater. You can also view the fireworks outside for free at Shoreline Park. mountainview.gov 🎉Napa: 4th of July parade 9:30 a.m. parade along 2nd and 3rd streets. Activities, vendors, performances, a drone show and more throughout the day. napa4thofjulyparade.com Pacifica: Fourth of July Celebration and Rocket Run 11 a.m. to 2 p.m. Located at Frontierland Park Enjoy food and drinks, games, rides, live music, and more. Rocket Run check-in begins at 8 a.m. and the run will start at 9 a.m. The first 75 people to arrive will receive a t-shirt. cityofpacifica.org Palo Alto: 4th of July Summer Festival and Chili Cook-Off 11 a.m. to 3 p.m. Located in Mitchell Park, people can come and enjoy food trucks, live music and — of course — the chili cook-off. cityofpaloalto.org 🎉Pittsburg: July 4th Fireworks Show 9 p.m. to 11 p.m. Located at the Pittsburg Marina. The event includes fireworks, food trucks, and a Waterfront Firework Party. Join in for a celebration of America 250. Space is limited so arrive early. pittsburgca.gov Pleasant Hill: Celebrating the 4th of July Events throughout the day; 9:30 a.m. parade, 8:45 p.m. fireworks show. The viewing party for fireworks returns to College Park and fields open at 6:30 p.m. $4 per person suggested donation. phjuly4.com 🎉Redwood City: Family 4th of July Parade Pancake breakfast at 8 a.m. Parade at 10 a.m., then a festival showcasing local organizations and artists. Drone show at 9:30 p.m. over the Port of Redwood City. This year’s theme is “America 250.” parade.org 🎉San Francisco: 7X7 Light Installation June 21 to July 4 at Civic Center Plaza Free public lights installation with a USA theme on July 4 sf.gov/7×7 🎉San Francisco: America 250 Picnic and Speeches 12 p.m. to 4 p.m. Located at Fort Mason Park. Opportunity to read a short, historic speech. Live music to accompany. Fireworks will be shown nearby in the evening. america250.org/san-francisco San Francisco: Fourth of July fireworks on Golden Gate Bridge Fireworks show will begin at 9:30 p.m. Recommended viewing locations include Crissy Field, Marina Green, Pier 39 and the northern Embarcadero. Attendees are advised to arrive early due to traffic and limited parking. sf.gov/july4 🎉San Jose: 4th of July Celebration at Lake Cunningham Festival starts at 6 p.m., Drone Show starts at 9 p.m. Located at Lake Cunningham Park. Food, live entertainment and activities for kids. sanjoseca.gov/4thofJuly San Jose: Rose, White and Blue parade Kickoff – 9:45 a.m. Parade – 10 a.m. to 12 p.m. next to Lincoln High School. Festival – 11 a.m. to 2 p.m. Parade, entertainment, food and more. Historic celebration of diversity, public spirit and the community’s floral/agricultural heritage. rwbsj.org 🎉San Leandro: 250th Independence Day Event 11 a.m. to 4 p.m. at Monarch Bay Golf Course There will be live music, artisan vendors, food trucks and activities for all ages. No fireworks will be included. sanleandro.org San Pablo: 4th of July Multi-Cultural Celebration Doors open at 5:30 p.m. Drone show starts at 9:30 p.m. Enjoy live entertainment, carnival games, food, resource tables and a drone show. sanpabloca.gov 🎉Santa Clara: 4th of July Community Celebration 5 p.m. to 10 p.m. Located at Mission College. Live entertainment, food and fun activities will be available. Fireworks will begin at 9:40 p.m. and can be viewed at Central Plaza or Parking Lot A. santaclaraca.gov/4thofJuly Sausalito: 4th of July festivities 10 a.m. parade starts in Old Town; 12 p.m. to 4 p.m. picnic at Dunphy Park. 9:15 p.m. fireworks show at Gabrielson Park. Enjoy live music from Top Shelf Classics while waiting for the fireworks. sausalito.gov 🎉South San Francisco: 4th of July Parade and Picnic 10 a.m. to 2 p.m. Parade starts at Orange Memorial Park and moves to City Hall. Celebrate America 250 with family-friendly activities, entertainment, giveaways and more. ssfca.gov 🎉Sunnyvale: 4th of July Festival 10 a.m. to 2 p.m. Located in Downtown Sunnyvale. Enjoy amusement rides, carnival games, slides, performances and more for America 250. There will be a bicycle parade at 9:30 a.m. sunnyvale.ca.gov 🎉Vallejo: Mare Island Spirit Ship Celebration 5:30 p.m. to 9:30 p.m. Featuring a 125 drone show by Sky Elements above the Mare Island waterfront. Includes food trucks, beverages, treats and kids’ games and activities. It is recommended to bring your own lawn chairs or blankets. america250.org/mare-island Vallejo: Star-Spangled Nights at Six Flags Discovery Kingdom 9:15 p.m. on Friday and Saturday. Watch the drone show from rides and spots across the theme park. sixflags.com

headlines.title: ‘We're behind you': Bruce Arena optimistic about USMNT at the World Cup
headlines.description: Bruce Arena, the current San Jose Earthquakes head coach and former U.S. men’s national team manager, said Thursday he believes this year’s American squad has a chance to go all the way at the World Cup. On Wednesday, the Stars and Stripes defeated Bosnia and Herzegovina 2-0 in a Round of 32 match in Santa Clara. The Americans will now head to Seattle to take on Belgium in the Round of 16 on Monday. “We’re behind you,” Arena said of the U.S. squad. “We wish you the best. I pick them as my sleeper team now at this point. There’s 16 teams left.” Arena, a soccer hall of famer, coached the U.S. men’s national soccer team when it reached the quarterfinals of the World Cup in 2002. He said the competition for Team USA at this year’s tournament is about to get tougher, but he has faith. NBC Bay Area’s Damian Trujillo has more in the video report above. Santa Clara 22 hours ago 2026 World Cup Jul 1 2026 World Cup 8 hours ago Can Folarin Balogun appeal his red card? What to know for USA-Belgium

headlines.title: USMNT win in Santa Clara brings pride to Americans across the nation
headlines.description: Just a couple of days away from the Fourth of July, many Americans have been bursting with pride for the country following one big event after another with the Olympics, the Super Bowl and the World Cup. The most recent success was the U.S. national team breaking through in the knockout round of the World Cup last night with a win over Bosnia. National pride is what the World Cup is all about, and as these fans of Spain will say, nothing brings it out more than a world stage. One fan says he saw it for himself Wednesday at the Team USA match against Bosnia at the San Francisco Bay Area Stadium. “It’s always great when the US wins. Exciting. What was really special was the amount of American fans; a lot of times I’ve been to matches and we’re outnumbered by the opponent’s fans, but this time it was it was wall to wall American fans, and it was great to see,” Fresno resident Marco Torres said. Another fan who watched the match at San Pedro Square says it’s more than just winning a game. “Being an American with the World Cup coming into town actually has been a very joyous occasion. To see the rest of the world come to us, and kind of see what we are outside of the news and all that, and so it’s kind of upped my patriotism a little bit and it’s been an exciting time to, sort of, celebrate the melting pot that is America with the whole world watching,” San Diego resident Quinn Hovey said. That rejuvenated pride showed at the FIFA store at San Jose’s Santana Row where the store is restocking after selling out its USA Merchandise Wednesday. “It’s very, very cool to see and be able to watch people walk in and look at the merch and say ‘Oh that’s so cool’ then come in and buy it,” FIFA store clerk L.J. Stendera said. Some customers say it’s nice to see the nation coming together. “Yeah, I definitely feel that ‘unity’, because there’s been a lot that’s been driving all of us apart. It’s been really nice to see ‘sport’ be a way to have all of us come together,” Livermore resident Melissa Landera said. It has been sports that have done that lately. Many people said it does feel like kind of a carryover from the Olympics to World Cup.

headlines.title: Newsom signs new law boosting oversight of state's troubled Next Gen 911 project
headlines.description: California Gov. Gavin Newsom signed a budget bill this week that strengthens oversight of the state’s troubled Next Generation 911 project by requiring an independent audit, quarterly reporting, and technical reviews. Lawmakers say the requirements will help get the digital 911 overhaul back on track after years of delays, technical failures, and cost overruns. The provisions were originally part of Assemblymember Rhodesia Ransom’s AB 1805 but were later folded into a budget bill signed into law Monday. The new accountability measures follow two years of reporting by NBC Bay Area’s Investigative Unit detailing project delays, technical problems, and allegations that state leaders and contractors endangered lives by deploying new technology in California emergency dispatch centers before it was proven to work effectively. Some of those concerns were later echoed in a Legislative Analyst’s Office report. The California Office of Emergency Services (Cal OES), the agency overseeing the Next Gen 911 project, also began documenting a laundry list of issues with the project on its website late last year. “We have spent hundreds of millions of taxpayer dollars on a project to keep Californians safe, and after more than a decade our state still has no reliable network to show for it,” Ransom said. “[The] budget deal changes the status quo and moves us towards the Next Generation of 911 services.” Assemblymember Ransom said NBC Bay Area’s reporting has helped elected officials piece together the timeline and impact of the problems. “You being able to document the real-life impact is very important part, and I definitely appreciate you for having done that and having stuck with what’s happening,” she said. “And I hope you continue to follow it because we want to make sure that we hold everyone accountable for doing the things that we just agreed to in this budget bill.” The state has since scrapped its original blueprint for the project and recently requested $141 million to implement a new plan that officials hope will have the Next Gen system up and running in Southern California in time for the LA Olympics games. The budget package, however, imposes new reporting and oversight requirements before the state can move ahead with spending those funds on a long-term statewide Next Gen 911 contract. Key features in the new budget include: An independent technical evaluation of the project A state audit by the California State Auditor Quarterly progress reports to the Legislature Additional authority for the state’s 911 Advisory Board The delay of new contracts until after the technical review The new measures go into effect immediately. “I was able to talk to the newly sworn in Cal OES director and having had that conversation with her, we are all on the same page that we need to learn lessons from what happened,” Ransom said. Cal OES said in a statement the agency was pleased to work collaboratively with the legislature. “This agreement moves the project forward while enacting common sense actions to ensure ratepayer funds continue to be expended wisely and effectively,” a Cal OES spokesperson wrote. The Investigative Unit’s reporting on California’s Next Generation 911 project can be viewed here: www.nbcbayarea.com/nextgen911

headlines.title: SF neighbors concerned after person allegedly steals sewer grates
headlines.description: A person was caught on camera lifting small sewer grates in San Francisco’s Bernal Height neighborhood and calmly walking away. Neighbors have been calling the city hoping to have authorities track down the culprit, but instead they’re getting cited with orders to replace the grates as soon as possible. The video that neighbors are circulating was shot on a door cam at about 3 a.m. on June 6. The suspect was caught on camera with a head worn lamp and a couple bags, as he goes behind a porta potty where the sewer vent in the sidewalk is. Neighbors say it only took him about 20 seconds to remove the sewer grate and walk off. “We can’t figure out whether it’s for profit, whether it’s for art,” San Francisco resident Jim Smith said. For most residents it’s become annoying, and a little dangerous. “My little dog could step in it. I could fall, trip and fall, any of us could do that,” San Francisco resident Sheila said. Along with sharing video of the man who they believe is doing this, residents have also been calling the city to report what happened. But the city is reportedly putting the blame on neighbors. One woman, who did not want to be identified, said city workers showed up, marked the missing grate area with white paint and then gave her a notice, ordering her to replace the grate. She described how she handled it. “At first, I put in a plastic one in. But I couldn’t get it in. And I didn’t think it would pass inspection,” she said. According to the city Public Utilities Commission and the Department of Public Works, the sidewalk in front of a house, including its grates, is the responsibility of the homeowner. A plumber said that these holes are intended to vent sewer gas, and the problem is when grate is gone, stuff starts collecting around here and can fall in, and cause problems for the system. Matt, with Real Plumbing and Heating, says he has a customer in the South of Market “She’s replaced it, and since then epoxied it down. And when currently servicing it, I had to screw it back down really tight. Because she was concerned it was going to disappear again,” Matt said. So far, none of the neighbors NBC Bay Area talked with have mentioned anything about the city making any effort to track down whoever is stealing the grates, but the Department of Public Works does have a list of hardware stores where residents can simply buy replacements. They run for about $10 to $20 each.

headlines.title: Telescope with Bay Area roots becomes part of project to scan the universe
headlines.description: An ambitious project to scan the universe has ties to the Bay Area. A telescope with roots in the Bay Area is be part of a project to look deep into space to go back into the history of the universe, as well as learn about what might be coming up in the future. The enormous telescope is inside the Vera Rubin Observatory in Chile. Nine years ago, it started getting partially being built at the SLAC National Laboratory in Menlo Park, and now, it is being used to map the night sky. “The Ruben Observatory is gonna cover one fifth of the observable sky every single night, and then do it again, and again, over 10 years,” Stanford and SLAC Professor Risa Wechsler said. The goal? Collect 10 terabytes of data a night for a decade from a 3,200-megapixel camera – the biggest digital camera in the world. “And to stand next to the telescope you really feel the magnitude of this project,” Kate Napier, a postdoctoral Observing Specialist, said. “We expect to observe 20 billion galaxies in our universe, and almost 20 billion stars in the Milky Way, and that is going to enable a wide range of different sciences,” Ruben Observatory Survey Deputy Aaron Roodman said. Science, like, how and why the universe is expanding and what stars are made of. It just kicked off what it calls a survey of space and time, looking for distant galaxies, as well as objects flying around between us and the sun. “And these are potentially the most dangerous objects, and they can sneak up on us quickly,” Ruben Observatory Calibration Scientist Eli Rykoff said.

headlines.title: Squatters take over home of slain UC Berkeley professor, family says
headlines.description: A year after a University of California, Berkeley professor was killed during a custody dispute in Greece, his family says they’re fighting to try to get alleged squatters out of his East Bay home. The family of Przemyslaw Jeziorski said the alleged squatters have been living in the Berkeley home for months, but California’s eviction process has made removing them far more complicated. Jeziorski was shot and killed in Greece last July in the middle of a custody battle. The boyfriend of Jeziorski’s ex-wife later confessed to the shooting. Jeziorski’s ex-wife, Nadia Michelidaki, died by suicide in a Greek prison this past May. The former couple’s home, used for Airbnb, is now occupied. Court records show the people living there claim they signed a one-year lease with Jeziorski’s ex-wife and paid $30,000 up front. Erinne Stratte, the Jeziorski family’s attorney, said that’s impossible because she was in prison when the lease was allegedly signed. “The homeowner just has to let the squatters stay, and based on their criminal background and the inconsistencies in their statements, it’s clear that they are not telling the truth,” Stratte said. Crime and Courts Jul 14, 2025 UC Berkeley Jul 16, 2025 Last December, Berkeley police found signs of a break-in and possible squatting. A woman later returned claiming she had a one-year lease. Stratte said the occupants have provided no proof of a lease or payment, and police reports show conflicting accounts of when they moved in. Despite eviction notices, they remain in the home. “If they are making an allegation, they gave money to someone, and then the law is requiring us to indemnify or prevent the squatters from harm in case someone else took advantage of them, which I see as a flaw in the law,” Stratte said NBC Bay Area went to the home and knocked on the door, but no one answered. The phone number listed in court records has been disconnected. The family alleges the occupants have been selling belongings from the home — something neighbors said they’ve seen at yard sales. Attorneys said the court process could take months. Meanwhile, Jeziorski’s brother, Lukasz Jeziorski, continues to raise the couple’s 11-year-old twins while paying the mortgage on the home the children are supposed to inherit. “They are stealing from the two orphans,” Lukasz said. “If you ask me what I think about them, I feel nothing good. I feel that they are very bad people.”

headlines.title: Members of unhoused community urge Antioch to approve housing project
headlines.description: Some people in Antioch who are unhoused are camping out at City Hall Thursday, because they say the city wants to clear them out for Fourth of July festivities, without providing them shelter or somewhere else to go. Members of Antioch’s unhoused community say they don’t want to be swept under the rug any longer. They’ve set up their tents and belongings outside city hall to send a message. “All we want is somewhere to lay our heads down somewhere safe, somewhere we feel like we belong, and they don’t care to give us that at all,” unhoused protester Chalonda Hill said. They said they were forced to clear their encampments along the railroad tracks on Wednesday, but when they moved to a nearby park to rest and figure out their next move, they said code enforcement tried to remove their belongings. That’s when Homeless Advocate Nichol Gardener showed up. She says the unsheltered have every right to use city parks during the day. “If I was here with a picnic basket and had my blanket, you wouldn’t bother me. You need to treat them the same way,” Gardener said. So, the group decided to protest in front of City Hall. They are urging the city to approve a proposed homeless housing project called Homekey Plus. There are currently no homeless shelters in Antioch. “We want the key project to pass and get us some housing. A lot of us have been on the streets for a while and all the police keep doing is coming and removing our belongings our personal belongings,” unhoused protester Michelle Rand said. The county’s core team is on site trying to find temporary shelter for the protesters and they’re handing out clean socks and other supplies. The city has given the campers notice they must move from City Hall by July 4, but the protesters say they aren’t budging. “They hurried up and just tried to throw everything away because it’s Fourth of July weekend and they have a huge fireworks over there so they wanted all of that to be perfect but it’s like what about us? We’re not important?” Hill said. “These folks right here sticking up for themselves. I’m just so proud. I’m really so proud of them,” Gardener said.

headlines.title: Coast Guard details safety plan for July Fourth Golden Gate Bridge fireworks show
headlines.description: The United States Coast Guard on Thursday outlined its plans for keeping people safe during the July Fourth fireworks show at the Golden Gate Bridge. “We fully anticipate to have a large crowd, both onshore and on the water,” U.S. Coast Guard Capt. Jared Toczko said. “This is an opportunity to see a fantastic fireworks display and with that we expect crowds to gather. Obviously, as vessels are positioning to witness the show, we ask that they abide by those safety zones.” The safety zones include the Golden Gate Bridge, the fireworks barge display east of the bridge and the barge display near Pier 39. “These zones are designated to keep all recreational and commercial vessels at a safe distance from the fireworks operations and to provide a clear lane for emergency response, if needed,” U.S. Coast Guard Lt. Cmdr. Eddie Kalankiewicz said. Drones will be banned over the firework venues and surrounding waterfront. NBC Bay Area’s Christie Smith has more in the video report above. Fourth of July Jun 26, 2025 San Francisco Jun 30 SF 4th of July fireworks: How to watch historic Golden Gate Bridge display

headlines.title: Water, sewage, garbage rates go up for residents in Sunnyvale
headlines.description: Utility bills are going up this week in Sunnyvale. The city’s new budget increases rates for water, sewage, garbage and recycling, with the city estimating that the typical family will pay close to $13 more each month. Before the budget passed last month, dozens of residents protested the changes, but the city says the rate hikes will offset rising costs for water and help replace old infrastructure.

headlines.title: SF 4th of July fireworks: How to watch historic Golden Gate Bridge display
headlines.description: San Francisco will celebrate America’s 250th birthday with a Fourth of July fireworks display from the Golden Gate Bridge. City officials said the display on the Golden Gate Bridge will be the third time in its 90-year history fireworks will be launched from the bridge. Here’s how to watch and what to know about Saturday’s fireworks show. When will the Golden Gate Bridge 4th of July fireworks begin? The Golden Gate Bridge display is free and will begin at approximately 9:30 p.m. The launch will occur from the east side of the Golden Gate Bridge. How to watch the San Francisco fireworks show online NBC Bay Area coverage of the 4th of July fireworks display in San Francisco will begin at 9 p.m. Saturday. A livestream will be available on this page in the video player above, so be sure to bookmark and check back then! Viewers can also stream the fireworks display on our 24/7 channel, NBC Bay Area News, on the NBC Bay Area app or by tapping the “Watch News 24/7” button on NBCBayArea.com. The channel is also available to stream on Roku TV, Peacock, Fire TV, Samsung TV Plus, Xumo Play, Local Now, Pluto, Google TV, TCL, Xfinity, and Amazon Prime Video. Fourth of July Jun 26, 2025 Fourth of July Jun 27 Fourth of July Jun 17 Where can the Golden Gate Bridge 4th of July fireworks be viewed in person? Recommended viewing locations include Crissy Field, Marina Green, Pier 39 and the northern Embarcadero. The fireworks can be viewed from additional vantage points on the east side of the bridge. Attendees are recommended to arrive early due to potential crowds. How will traffic be impacted during the Golden Gate Bridge 4th of July fireworks? Public transportation is also strongly advised. Muni will have an extended service to help travelers arrive home following the show. The Presidio is also accessible by public transit, including the free Presidio GO shuttle. Traffic in the Presidio and surrounding neighborhoods is highly anticipated. Visitors driving to the event should expect parking lots to fill up early, close when full, have special event fees and delayed exit times. Drivers planning to travel between San Francisco and Marin County are advised to seek alternative routes through the East Bay or adjust their travel times. Heavy congestion on U.S. 101 and Highway 1 are also anticipated. The Golden Gate Bridge and its sidewalks will be fully closed during the evening fireworks show. The bridge will close to drivers at 8:30 p.m. and will reopen shortly after the conclusion of the display. The east sidewalk will be closed from Friday, July 3 at 5 a.m. to Sunday, July 5 at 5 a.m. to allow for pyrotechnic staging and removal. The west sidewalk will be closed on Saturday, July 4 from 8 p.m. to 10 p.m., reopening for bicyclists only. For more information on the event, visit sf.gov/july4.

headlines.title: Protesters who shut down Golden Gate Bridge found guilty of false imprisonment
headlines.description: Seven pro-Palestinian protesters who shut down traffic on the Golden Gate Bridge in 2024 to denounce U.S. aid to Israel in the Israel-Hamas war were all found guilty of false imprisonment by a jury in San Francisco on Thursday. One by one, the court clerk first read out guilty verdicts for each of the counts for each defendant. Supporters of the protesters were stunned after each verdict was read out as “guilty,” with some people crying quietly. All seven protesters were found guilty of false imprisonment, obstruction of a thoroughfare, and unlawful assembly. As the verdicts were read out, some defendants remained stoic while others cried. Those convicted include six who chained themselves to cars on the bridge: Conrad de Jesus, Rocky Chau, Sarah Ferrell, River Allen, Em Tillotson, and Bhavika Anandpura. Sara Cantor, who described herself as a police liaison, was also convicted. Cantor was found guilty of the same charges in addition to a charge of refusal to disperse. The jury remained “hopelessly deadlocked” on two counts for trespassing and conspiracy. For the trespassing counts, the jury ended on 11-1 for not guilty. The jury was deadlocked 10-2 for guilty on the conspiracy charge. San Francisco Superior Court Judge Teresa Caffese declared a mistrial for the two counts. Caffese thanked the jurors, who began deliberations three weeks ago. “You have now completed your jury service in this case,” she said. “On behalf of all the judges of the court, please accept my thanks for your time and effort … You’ve been incredible.” As the courtroom emptied, supporters of the protesters chanted “Free Palestine!” and “Shame!” The crowd continued chanting outside the courtroom, yelling “From the river to the sea, Palestine will be free,” through tears, sadness, and anger.
